description:
You are an integration-developer validating latency on a small set of test-API calls.  
    A raw CSV-style log already exists on the system:

        /home/user/api_test/logs/api_responses_20240314.log

    The file contains **exactly five (5) lines, no header row, comma separated**.  
    Each line has five (5) columns in this order:

        1. ISO-8601 timestamp (no spaces)
        2. HTTP method
        3. Endpoint path
        4. Numeric status code
        5. Response time ending with the literal text “ms” (e.g. 123ms)

    Example line format (spacing shown only for clarity):
        2024-03-14T07:21:01Z , GET , /users , 200 , 115ms

    GOAL  
    ----  
    1. Inspect the file and create a *new* CSV file named  

           /home/user/api_test/reports/slow_calls.csv  

       that contains just two columns:  
           • timestamp (column-1 from the source file)  
           • endpoint  (column-3 from the source file)

    2. The new file must include a **single header row** followed by one line for every request
       whose response time is **100 ms or greater**.

    3. Use classic Unix column manipulation tools such as `cut`, `paste`, `grep`, `awk`,
       or any equivalent combination, but keep the solution to roughly three or four commands.
       Do NOT edit the source log manually.

    Output specification that the automated grader will check
    ----------------------------------------------------------
    The target file **/home/user/api_test/reports/slow_calls.csv** must exist and contain exactly the text shown below (final newline required):

        timestamp,endpoint
        2024-03-14T07:21:01Z,/users
        2024-03-14T07:23:50Z,/users/42
        2024-03-14T07:24:30Z,/reports

    Directory notes
    ---------------
    • Create the directory /home/user/api_test/reports/ if it does not already exist.  
    • You do not need root access for any step.  
    • Only the final CSV file will be graded; you may leave behind any temporary
      files you create.

    Hints
    -----
    • `cut -d',' -f?` is useful for extracting specific columns.  
    • `grep -E` or `awk` can be used to filter on “[1-9][0-9][0-9]?ms”.  
    • `paste -d','` can rejoin columns if you extract them separately.  

    Wrap-up
    -------
    When finished, you should have:

        /home/user/api_test/reports/slow_calls.csv   ← exactly matching the
                                                      “Output specification” block above.

    No additional console output is required, but feel free to display your work for your own verification.
truth:
Initial filesystem state
    ------------------------
    1. Directory /home/user/api_test/logs/ already exists with permissions 755.
    2. Directory /home/user/api_test/reports/ does NOT exist yet.
    3. The file /home/user/api_test/logs/api_responses_20240314.log exists with permissions 644 and the
       following exact contents (each line terminated by a single newline, LF):

        2024-03-14T07:21:01Z,GET,/users,200,115ms
        2024-03-14T07:22:45Z,POST,/login,201,89ms
        2024-03-14T07:23:50Z,DELETE,/users/42,204,102ms
        2024-03-14T07:24:30Z,GET,/reports,500,321ms
        2024-03-14T07:25:10Z,PUT,/users/42,404,78ms

    Expected end state
    ------------------
    After the agent completes the task, the grader will verify:

    1. Directory /home/user/api_test/reports/ exists, permissions 755 or more restrictive.
    2. File /home/user/api_test/reports/slow_calls.csv exists, permissions 644 (any non-executable).
    3. The file /home/user/api_test/reports/slow_calls.csv contains exactly six (6) lines including the
       terminating newline and **matches byte-for-byte** the text:

        timestamp,endpoint
        2024-03-14T07:21:01Z,/users
        2024-03-14T07:23:50Z,/users/42
        2024-03-14T07:24:30Z,/reports

       (i.e., header plus three qualifying entries, each line ending in LF).

    No other files are required for grading.
